summary: Inequalities solve almost exactly like equations, with one critical rule: multiplying or dividing by a negative number flips the inequality sign.

formulas.name: Sign flip rule
formulas.expression: \text{if } c < 0 \text{, then } a < b \iff ac > bc

explanation: Solve linear inequalities the same way as equations — add, subtract, multiply, or divide both sides — with one key exception: multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number reverses the direction of the inequality. For example, \(-2x < 6\) becomes \(x > -3\) (the sign flips) when dividing by \(-2\). Solutions to an inequality are typically a range of values, not a single number, and are often represented on a number line or in interval notation. When a word problem asks for the greatest or least possible integer value satisfying an inequality, solve the inequality first, then round appropriately in the direction the inequality allows.
